attributes.stem:
An 83-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with worsening confusion over the past two days. He has a medical history of metastatic prostate cancer with known bone metastases and chronic kidney disease. His regular medications include extended and instant release oral morphine for pain management. He has had a recent diarrhoeal illness.

On examination, he appears confused and dehydrated. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. His mucous membranes are dry, and his skin turgor is reduced. He does not appear to be in any discomfort.

Investigations reveal:

  • Creatinine: 380 µmol/L (60-110)

  • eGFR: 16 mL/min/1.73m2 (>90)

  • White cell count: 14 x 10^9/L (4-11)

  • C-reactive protein: 80 mg/L (<5)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents an 83-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer and chronic kidney disease who has developed acute-on-chronic kidney injury (AKI) and confusion in the context of a recent diarrhoeal illness and likely dehydration. His blood tests show a significantly elevated creatinine and reduced eGFR, indicating worsening of his pre-existing renal impairment, as well as raised inflammatory markers. There are multiple factors contributing to his delirium ; possible viral gastrointestinal illness, opioid accumulation in the context of an acute kidney injury and dehydration principal amongst the causative factors.

Commencing intravenous fluid resuscitation is the most appropriate next step in this situation, despite the severity of the kidney injury. The patient shows signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia, and hypotension), which is likely contributing to his acute-on-chronic kidney injury and confusion. Careful fluid resuscitation can potentially improve renal function and may also improve his cognitive status. Even in palliative care patients with pre-existing chronic kidney disease, reversible causes of deterioration should be addressed if consistent with the patient's goals of care.

Administering naloxone is not the first step here, although opioid toxicity should be strongly considered given the severe renal impairment. The patient's confusion could be due to opioid accumulation secondary to AKI, but addressing the underlying dehydration first is more appropriate. If confusion persists after fluid resuscitation, naloxone could be considered. There are also at present on examination limited signs of opioid toxicity.

Arranging urgent dialysis might be necessary if the patient doesn't respond to initial fluid resuscitation, but it's not the first step, and shouldn't be routinely considered in a patient with metastatic cancer. The decision to initiate dialysis in a palliative care patient with severe AKI should be made carefully, considering the patient's overall prognosis and goals of care. Attempting fluid resuscitation first is a less invasive approach that may improve the situation.

Withholding oral morphine would be inappropriate at this stage, without switching to an alternative agent better tolerated in renal failure, such as hydromorphone. While the patient's confusion may be partly due to opioid accumulation, abruptly stopping pain medication in a patient with metastatic cancer could lead to uncontrolled pain. The priority is to address the dehydration and improve renal function, which may help with opioid clearance, and then consider reinitiating an appropriate agent.

Commencing broad-spectrum antibiotics might be considered if there were clear signs of infection, but the current presentation is more consistent with dehydration following a diarrhoeal illness. The elevated white cell count and CRP could be due to the stress response or the underlying cancer, or a viral gastroenteritis. Antibiotics may be considered later if signs of infection persist after initial fluid resuscitation.

In managing palliative care patients with acute issues and chronic kidney disease, it's crucial to balance treating reversible causes with overall goals of care. In this case, addressing the dehydration and acute-on-chronic kidney injury with fluid resuscitation is a reasonable first step that may significantly improve the patient's condition without subjecting him to overly invasive interventions. However, close monitoring is essential, and the treatment plan may need to be adjusted based on the patient's response and overall goals of care.
learningPoints: Acute-on-chronic kidney injury in palliative care patients requires careful assessment of reversibility and patient goals.
Opioid toxicity can occur in the setting of dehydration and renal impairment due to reduced clearance.
Careful fluid resuscitation can improve renal function and cognition in selected palliative care patients, even with pre-existing chronic kidney disease.
references: Liu S, Sands MB, Kerferd JW, Fathabadi S, Khor KE, Yiu CH, Phinn K, Anderson MA, Redmayne G, Yong K, Penm J. Opioid Analgesic Use among Patients with Kidney Disease: A Systematic Review. Kidney and Blood Pressure Research. 2024 May 23;49(1):495-512. Available online ; https://karger.com/kbr/article/49/1/495/908829/Opioid-Analgesic-Use-among-Patients-with-Kidney

Davison SN, Pommer W, Brown MA, Douglas CA, Gelfand SL, Gueco IP, Hole BD, Homma S, Kazancıoğlu RT, Kitamura H, Koubar SH. Conservative kidney management and kidney supportive care: core components of integrated care for people with kidney failure. Kidney international. 2024 Jan 1;105(1):35-45. Available online at ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S008525382300683X

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
